item.title: Individual decadent chocolate cakes
item.description: Two 4-inch chocolate cakes built on a chocolate-butter emulsion and bloomed Dutch cocoa, with brown sugar for moisture and a turbinado crackle top. No frosting: the cake itself is the payload.

item.title: Chocolate-butter emulsion
item.description: Melt chocolate and butter together into a single glossy mass before any egg, sugar, or flour. The cocoa butter and dairy fat fuse into one stable fat phase, which becomes the structural and flavor backbone of the bake.

item.title: Century egg (皮蛋, pidan)
item.description: Duck (or chicken, quail) egg preserved by alkaline cure for weeks to months. Black-amber translucent white, dark green creamy yolk, sulfurous-savory aroma. The cure does the cooking.

item.title: Jasmine rice
item.description: Long-grain aromatic rice (Oryza sativa indica) from Thailand and adjacent regions. Floral, soft, slightly clingy, around 18% amylose.

item.title: Salted duck egg (咸鸭蛋, xian ya dan)
item.description: Raw duck egg cured in salt brine for thirty to forty days. White stays mostly liquid and saline, yolk turns dense, deep orange-red, oil-bleeding, fudgy. A different ingredient from a century egg.

item.title: Cantonese white congee
item.description: A neutral base of rice cooked at a tremor for an hour into a creamy, near-cream consistency. The rice pretreatment is the technique: oil-and-salt massage, or freeze-then-simmer, breaks the grain so it surrenders its starch fully.

item.title: Chicken and ginger congee
item.description: The healing-food version. Bone-in chicken thighs simmered into the rice, a heavy ginger load to clean the palate, finished with sesame oil, scallion, and chili oil at the table. The Cantonese kitchen calls this comfort food when someone is sick.

item.title: Crispy fried shallots (and the shallot oil)
item.description: Thinly sliced shallots fried slow in neutral oil until deep gold and shatteringly crisp. The oil that comes free is the bonus ingredient and arguably more useful than the shallots themselves.

item.title: Cured egg yolk (salt-sugar dry cure)
item.description: Raw yolks buried in a 70:30 salt-sugar bed for a week, rinsed, then dried in a low oven or dehydrator until they grate like a hard cheese. Translucent, deep amber, dense umami concentrate.

item.title: Ginger-scallion oil (薑蔥油, geung chung yau)
item.description: Cantonese aromatic finishing oil. Minced ginger and scallion shocked with smoking-hot neutral oil into a paste that perfumes plain rice, congee, poached chicken, steamed fish, and noodles.

item.title: Pork and century egg congee (皮蛋瘦肉粥)
item.description: The canonical Cantonese savory congee. Bone-in pork shoulder simmered into the rice provides its own stock; cured pidan goes in twice, half during the simmer to bloom into the broth and half at the bowl for the textural contrast.

item.title: Basmati rice
item.description: Long-grain aromatic rice from the Indian subcontinent, low in amylopectin and high in 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline. Grains stay separate, lengthen dramatically on cooking, and carry a popcorn-pandan aroma that defines the cuisine it anchors.

item.title: Jameed
item.description: Fermented sheep or goat yogurt, salted and sun-dried into stone-hard cones. Reconstitutes into a sour, funky, glutamate-heavy sauce base that no live yogurt approximates.

item.title: Lamb shoulder (bone-in)
item.description: Collagen-rich, fat-marbled forequarter cut from young sheep. Built for slow-braise temperatures where the connective tissue hydrolyzes into gelatin and the meat falls off the bone.

item.title: Samna (clarified butter)
item.description: Butter cooked past its water phase and strained free of milk solids — pure butterfat, around 99% lipid, with the toasted-solid aroma already baked in. The Levantine and North African analog of Indian ghee.

item.title: Mansaf
item.description: Bone-in lamb shoulder simmered for hours, then bathed in a sauce built from reconstituted jameed (fermented dried sheep yogurt). Served over saffron-tinted basmati rice on a base layer of paper-thin shrak bread, crowned with samna-toasted pine nuts and almonds. The Bedouin and Jordanian feast dish, technique-faithful.

item.title: Cooked yogurt sauce
item.description: Stabilize yogurt with starch or egg, temper into the hot dish, and hold at a tremor below the boil. Casein curdles above 85 °C without help; the help is what makes a yogurt-based stew possible.

item.title: Cross-Contamination and the Danger Zone
item.description: The 2-hour and 4-hour rules for food in the 40-140°F danger zone, board and tool separation, marinade reuse, leftover cooling, and reheat targets. The orthogonal axis to internal-temperature targets.

item.title: Egg Doneness and Salmonella
item.description: Shell-egg pathogen risk math, when raw or partly cooked yolks are reasonable, and when to reach for pasteurized eggs. Covers carbonara, mayo, hollandaise, mousse, custards, and meringue.

item.title: Meat Doneness and Pasteurization
item.description: USDA published-safe internal temperatures for poultry, pork, beef, and lamb, plus the full time-temperature pasteurization table that explains why thin-sliced and sous-vide approaches can sit below the instantaneous number and still clear safety.

item.title: Seafood Doneness, Parasites, and Live Handling
item.description: Internal-temperature targets for fin fish and shellfish, parasite-freezing rules for raw applications, bivalve live-handling, scombroid and ciguatera basics, and what "sushi-grade" actually means.

item.title: Velveting
item.description: Toss thin-sliced lean protein with salt, baking soda, and a cornstarch slurry, rest 20-30 minutes, then cook briefly in a hot wet medium. The pH shift and gel jacket keep the muscle tender at temperatures that would otherwise wreck it.

item.title: Thai kitchen
item.description: Build the paste from scratch, then cook with it. The aromatics are the same across both recipes — making the paste first makes the curry legible.

item.title: Classic pancakes
item.description: Whole milk soured with vinegar replaces buttermilk — the acid drives CO2 from baking soda and pushes pH high enough for rapid Maillard browning. The result is tall, tender stacks with a caramelized underside.

item.title: Fresh egg pasta
item.description: Tipo 00 flour and whole eggs, kneaded to silk and rolled thin. The base for tonnarelli, tagliatelle, pappardelle: anywhere a starchy, porous fresh sheet belongs. The gluten structure here is fundamentally different from dried pasta: lower protein, no durum, no extrusion.

item.title: Thai green curry paste
item.description: A pounded-fresh Central Thai green curry paste: green chilies, lemongrass, galangal, makrut lime zest, coriander root, garlic, shallot, shrimp paste. Fresher, oilier, and more aromatic than jarred. The difference is cell-wall rupture: a mortar achieves it and a blade does not.

item.title: Acid-milk souring
item.description: Add a food acid (vinegar or lemon juice) to whole milk — casein micelles destabilize, viscosity rises, and the liquid is now functionally equivalent to buttermilk for leavening and batter behavior.

item.title: All-purpose flour
item.description: Wheat flour at 10–12% protein. The middle of the wheat-flour band: enough gluten potential for chew, soft enough for tender crumbs when handled gently.

item.title: Black peppercorns
item.description: The unripe drupes of Piper nigrum, dried whole. Heat is piperine (fat-soluble); aroma is a stack of volatile terpenes that vanish within minutes of grinding.

item.title: Broccoli
item.description: Brassica oleracea var. italica. Sulfur-forward when overcooked; nutty and sweet with a hard, dry sear.
